_source: Household systems & appliances: manufacturer owner's-manual guidance aggregated by the U.S. Department of Energy 'Energy Saver' maintenance checklists (energy.gov) and ENERGY STAR HVAC maintenance guidance (filter changes, annual tune-ups); NFPA 211 / U.S. Fire Administration guidance on annual dryer-vent cleaning (fire-prevention basis); National Roofing Contractors Association (NRCA) and This Old House guidance on annual roof inspection and twice-yearly (spring/fall) gutter cleaning; typical manufacturer care-guide intervals (GE, Whirlpool, Samsung, LG) for refrigerator coil cleaning and water-filter replacement, dishwasher filter/spray-arm cleaning, and washer hose/gasket checks. Cost ranges are 2024-2025 U.S. national-average service-call ranges per Angi and HomeAdvisor cost guides — treat as ballpark, not a quote. Vehicle tasks are powertrain-specific (see docs/decisions.md ADR-012): gas cars follow typical manufacturer intervals (5,000-7,500 mi conventional oil, 6,000 mi tire rotation, annual brake/battery/cabin-filter check), hybrids run the engine less so oil intervals stretch (synthetic, ~annual), and battery-electric vehicles have NO engine oil, spark plugs, or emissions test — their schedule is tire rotation (EVs are heavy with instant torque, so tires wear faster), cabin filter, brake-fluid service (regenerative braking spares the pads), 12V battery, and periodic high-voltage battery coolant service per the maker (Tesla, etc.). Mileage intervals are approximated to calendar cadence using the FHWA average of ~13,500 miles/year; Phase 1 schedules are calendar-based only (see docs/ontology.md). Plug-in hybrids (PHEV) still have a gas engine and use the hybrid schedule. Vehicle service cost ranges per RepairPal and Angi national averages. These are editable defaults, not authoritative recommendations for a specific VIN.

water_heater.description: DIY drain is free; a plumber service call typically runs $75-$150

refrigerator.description: DIY vacuum is free; a service call runs roughly $75-$150

washer.description: Preventive replacement reduces flood risk even if hoses look fine

dryer.description: Lint-fire prevention (NFPA 211 / U.S. Fire Administration guidance)

vehicle_gas.description: Only applies where your state/locality requires it

vehicle_hybrid.description: Hybrids run the gas engine less and use synthetic oil, so the interval is typically longer than a conventional car
Regenerative braking spares the pads, but still inspect annually
Only applies where your state/locality requires it

vehicle_electric.description: EVs are heavy and make instant torque, so tires wear faster — rotate on schedule
Regenerative braking means pads rarely wear out, but the brake fluid still needs periodic replacement
EVs still have a 12V accessory battery separate from the drive battery
Per manufacturer schedule (e.g. Tesla) — no engine oil, spark plugs, or emissions test on an EV
Where required; EVs are exempt from tailpipe emissions testing
